readings.source: Argus-linked reporting (3 Jul) confirms continued falls; physical indications $450/t (17 Jun) → $420/t (18 Jun). Indicative — exact 6 Jul Egypt assessment is subscription-only.
June Yara–Mosaic contract confirmed at $775/t CFR, down $50/t from May's $825. No July contract agreed as of 3 Jul (monthly benchmark).
CME July settlement $750/st (2 Jul), down from $782.50 (25 Jun), ≈ −4.2% w/w. Aug $740, Sep $737.50.
Public indications ~$405–410/t CFR; a separate series $412.50 (25 Jun). Indicative, not exchange-traded.
Front-month ≈ €44.45/MWh (6 Jul), intraday €44.34–45.49; €42.57 on 29 Jun → ≈ +4.4% w/w (easing from Fri €45.10 close).

editorialReading: The Hormuz war premium is draining out of fertilizer. Egypt FOB granular urea — which spiked toward $800/t in May on Strait-of-Hormuz fear — has roughly halved to $420–450/t as the broader ceasefire remains formally in place and Gulf supply normalises (the fall is part fading risk premium, part post-spring demand lull) — though a new tanker strike near Oman has renewed concern over safe passage through Hormuz. Ammonia's June Tampa contract eased to $775/t (down $50 on May), DAP is drifting lower along the CME curve ($750/st July → $737.50 September), and potash is holding firm near $405–415/t. The exception — and the more durable European risk — is TTF gas: the front month has climbed back to ~€44.5/MWh, up ~4.4% on the week. TTF, not the war premium, is the lasting driver of European ammonia economics — when gas is dear, BASF, Yara and OCI idle EU plants and import finished product. So the acute, war-driven spike is unwinding even as the chronic gas-cost pressure quietly rebuilds.
watchNext: Whether the urea slide extends or finds a floor near the pre-war ~$390–420/t area, and whether a July Tampa ammonia contract settles below June's $775/t. Structurally, watch TTF: a sustained move above ~€45/MWh into the autumn 2026 application window would revive the idle-and-import reflex just as EU farmers buy for the season. India / China urea tender activity and any export curbs remain the physical swing factor. Sourcing note: real-time Argus / ICIS / Green Markets assessments are subscription-only and the free World Bank Pink Sheet lags ~a month — these are publicly-cited indications, and the urea figure in particular is indicative, not a confirmed 6 July assessment.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
